metadata.title: Build a Pareto Model-Selector with Claude Code

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The leaderboard winner missing my deadline at 400 milliseconds finally taught me that benchmark accuracy optimizes for the benchmark's constraints, not mine. Using accuracy, latency, and flash as three axes: (1) walk me through why a model is dominated only when another beats it on every axis at once, (2) show me how adding a 250-millisecond deadline mask greys out survivors that the unconstrained frontier still kept, (3) tell me how the frontier shifts if I weight flash far more heavily than latency for a tiny MCU.

props.command: The leaderboard winner missing my deadline at 400 milliseconds finally taught me that benchmark accuracy optimizes for the benchmark's constraints, not mine. Using accuracy, latency, and flash as three axes: (1) walk me through why a model is dominated only when another beats it on every axis at once, (2) show me how adding a 250-millisecond deadline mask greys out survivors that the unconstrained frontier still kept, (3) tell me how the frontier shifts if I weight flash far more heavily than latency for a tiny MCU.
props.segment: Build a Pareto Model-Selector with Claude

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes -- BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: The benchmark accuracy lies to you. The leaderboard winner has ninety-four percent and tops every chart. Put it on your device -- it runs in four hundred milliseconds and misses the deadline. A model at ninety percent fits in sixty milliseconds. You would have picked it, if you had plotted the whole field. The leaderboard optimizes for the leaderboard's constraints, not yours. The Pareto frontier is what model selection actually is.
beats.visual_intent: title card: leaderboard winner fails on device; Pareto frontier as the correct shortlist tool

beats.narration_text: Ask for the experiment. In the terminal: claude -- write pareto.py, given N models with accuracy, latency in milliseconds, and flash in kilobytes, compute the Pareto frontier and mark it, then grey out the dominated models.

props.command:
claude "write pareto.py: given N models with
  (accuracy, latency_ms, flash_kb),
  compute the Pareto frontier and mark it;
  grey out dominated models."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. A model is dominated if another candidate beats it on every axis simultaneously -- higher accuracy, lower latency, smaller flash. The frontier is the set no other model dominates. The leaderboard winner often falls off the frontier once latency and flash enter the comparison.

props.code:
# pareto.py  --  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

def is_dominated(model, candidates):
    # dominated if another beats it on ALL axes
    acc, lat, flash = model
    for c in candidates:
        if c is model: continue
        if (c[0] >= acc and c[1] <= lat
                and c[2] <= flash
                and c != model):
            return True
    return False

def pareto_frontier(models):
    # models: list of (accuracy, latency_ms, flash_kb)
    frontier = [m for m in models
                if not is_dominated(m, models)]
    dominated = [m for m in models if m not in frontier]
    return frontier, dominated

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot -- Manim video (illustrative data)
beats.narration_text: Run it. Eight models appear on the accuracy versus latency plane. One by one, the dominated models transform to grey -- F, G, and H each lose because another model beats them on every axis. The Pareto frontier draws itself through the survivors: C, B, A, D, and E. The leaderboard winner -- Model E at ninety-four percent -- survives, but only because nothing else beats it on all three axes.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: scatter plot, 8 model dots appear (GrowFromCenter), dominated ones transform to SLATE, Pareto frontier line draws through survivors.

beats.narration_text: The frontier with no constraints still includes models you can't use. Apply a deadline. Iterate: claude -- add a constraint mask, grey out anything over two hundred and fifty milliseconds before computing the frontier.

props.command:
claude "add a constraint mask --
  grey out anything over 250ms or 2MB
  before computing the frontier"

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot -- Manim video (illustrative data)
beats.narration_text: Re-run with the two-fifty millisecond cap. Model E at four hundred milliseconds transforms to grey -- constraint violation. The new frontier recomputes from the remaining models. The leaderboard winner is now off the shortlist. The frontier you actually use is smaller, and every model on it fits your deadline.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: same plane, 250ms vertical constraint line appears, Model E greys out, frontier redraws through remaining survivors. Chip: CONSTRAINT: 250ms CAP.

beats.narration_text: A single-number benchmark optimizes for the benchmark's constraints. The Pareto frontier optimizes for yours. Dominated models are eliminated before you spend time evaluating them. Constraint masks cut the frontier to what can actually ship. This is what model selection is.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: two rows -- single benchmark: wrong constraints; Pareto frontier: your constraints

beats.narration_text: Your move: collect the real accuracy, latency, and flash numbers for your candidate models -- accuracy from your eval set, latency and flash measured on the target hardware -- and run pareto.py on them. The output beats in this video use illustrative numbers. Swap in your measurements to get the frontier that actually applies to your device.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) eval accuracy, 2) measure on-target latency + flash, 3) run pareto.py

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. A single-number benchmark optimizes for the benchmark's constraints. The Pareto frontier optimizes for yours. Dominated models are eliminated before you spend time evaluating them. Constraint masks cut the frontier to what can actually ship. This is what model selection is. Your move: collect the real accuracy, latency, and flash numbers for your candidate models -- accuracy from your eval set, latency and flash measured on the target hardware -- and run pareto.py on them. The output beats in this video use illustrative numbers. Swap in your measurements to get the frontier that actually applies to your device.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
